narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile, including your early academic deciles, LSAT score, family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time, and it calculated a 57.4% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the average of about 5.7, signaling a weaker start compared to peers who typically perform better early on. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success because it exceeds what most applicants bring, showing your college-level strength. Rounding out the top influences, your Year 3 decile of 2—still below the average around 5.5—further nudged the model toward concern, as later academic performance is a key barometer for law school rigor. While your LSAT of 32 is below the 36.7 average and your lower-middle family income bracket differs from typical applicants, along with your full-time status matching the vast majority, these played lesser roles here. Overall, your profile stacks up as promising in some areas like GPA but hampered by early and mid-undergrad struggles relative to students who pass the bar at higher rates. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from your background—many in similar situations rebound strongly—but the model highlights patterns from past data where early decile weaknesses often predict bar challenges. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average, strengthening recent academic showings through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters that address your early hurdles. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds favorably, opening doors to law school and bar success.
